method3.generated_article:
==Name==
The name “Brecqhou” refers specifically to the island, whereas “Sark” may refer both to the island of Sark itself and to the wider Sark jurisdiction that includes Brecqhou and nearby islets and rocks.[1] The island is also recorded under the alternative form “Brechou”.[4] After Jeanne de Chesney married Denis Le Marchant of Guernsey, ownership passed into the Le Marchant family and the island became known as “Isle des Marchants”.[2] That historical name continued to be used even after ownership later passed to other owners.[2]

==Geography==
Brecqhou is a small island in the Channel Islands, a group in the English Channel off the coast of Normandy, within the Bailiwick of Guernsey.[2][5] It lies immediately adjacent to Great Sark and is separated from it by the narrow Le Gouliot Channel.[3] Its local position places it near Sark, which lies about 7 miles (11 km) east of Guernsey and about 25 miles (40 km) west of the Cherbourg Peninsula of France.[3] Brecqhou is one of the lesser inhabited islands of the Channel Islands, alongside islands such as Jethou and Lihou.[2] Geologically, it forms part of the ancient rock assemblage of the Bailiwick of Guernsey, with metamorphic rocks—principally schists and gneisses—forming most of Sark and Brecqhou.[4] These rocks record deformation and metamorphism dating to the early Proterozoic, around 2,020 million years ago.[4]

==Flag and stamps==
Flags and postage stamps associated with Brecqhou have been discussed as examples of how small islands in the Bailiwick of Guernsey use civic symbols to express local identity and jurisdictional distinctiveness.[2] Johnson places Brecqhou within a wider Bailiwick pattern in which islands and island jurisdictions have used symbols such as flags, crests, currency and postage stamps in ways comparable to nation-building practices.[2] In Brecqhou’s case, such symbolism is closely connected with the island’s political relationship with Sark and with disputes over space, ownership and authority between the two islands.[3] The use of stamps as political and cultural markers also sits within the broader postal history of the Bailiwick, where Guernsey’s postal independence from 1969 enabled distinctive local stamp issues.[5]

==Public visits==
Brecqhou is a privately owned island off the west coast of Sark, and public access is restricted.[1][3] Since the island was sold to the Barclay brothers in 1993, visits have been by invitation only.[3] The island is primarily associated with the Barclay family residence and a small number of island employees, rather than general tourism.[2][3] Its access arrangements differ from those of other nearby Channel Islands such as Herm, which is described as open to the public.[1]

==Notes and references==
Brecqhou is a small island of about 160 acres, lying roughly 85 metres west of Sark and forming part of Sark’s jurisdiction within the Bailiwick of Guernsey.[1]  

The Bailiwick of Guernsey comprises three jurisdictions—Guernsey, Sark and Alderney—and Sark’s jurisdiction includes Brecqhou.[4]  

Official and legal usage may treat “Sark” as referring both to the island of Sark itself and to the wider jurisdiction that includes Brecqhou and nearby islets and rocks.[5]  

The name Brecqhou is derived from Old Norse, commonly explained from elements meaning “escarpment” and “islet”, and the island may have served as a Viking base for raids in the early medieval period.[2]  

The suffix “-hou” also appears in the names of several nearby Channel Island and Norman islets, including Jethou, Lihou, Burhou and Tatihou.[4]  

Brecqhou has historically been treated as a tenement or dependency associated with Sark, although its precise status and relationship to Sark have been disputed in modern commentary.[5]  

Legal correspondence concerning the island’s sale in 1949 described Brecqhou as a dependency of Sark and stated that its tenure was similar to other Sark tenements, with the owner being tenant in perpetuity.[5]  

The island’s relationship with Sark has been politically contested, especially in the late twentieth century, when one commentator described Brecqhou as “effectively declaring UDI”.[3]  

Expressions of separate identity have included a Brecqhou flag made in the 1960s by Leonard Matchan, based on the Sark flag but incorporating Matchan’s coat of arms.[1]  

Brecqhou has also issued stamps or carriage labels, including issues by Matchan in 1969 and later issues by subsequent owners.[1]

==Feudal relationship with Sark==
Constitutionally, Brecqhou has generally been treated as an island tenement within the fief of Sark, with Chief Pleas historically exercising legislative authority over it, although some Sark laws have been disapplied there and its status has therefore been a recurring source of dispute.[2]

The basis of Sark’s feudal system was the 1565 grant by Elizabeth I to Helier de Carteret, made on condition that he defend the island, after which Sark was divided into forty tenements carrying enduring property rights and duties.[3] Brecqhou’s relationship to that settlement has been contested because it was not one of the original tenements, with one interpretation holding that its proximity and defensive significance brought it within the fief, while another argues that it was only later claimed as part of Sark.[2] Modern official descriptions have nevertheless treated Sark as a jurisdiction that includes Brecqhou, reinforcing the view that Sark law applies there subject to specific exceptions.[2]

The island’s feudal position became especially controversial after David and Frederick Barclay acquired Brecqhou in 1993, since Sark’s system made them liable to pay the treizième, a thirteenth of the purchase price, to the Seigneur.[3] The Barclays challenged aspects of seigneurial authority and Sark’s feudal institutions, helping to overturn male-dominated inheritance rules and contributing to the 2007 replacement of the treizième with a property transfer tax payable to the island.[3] Their wider campaign was linked to the constitutional reform of Sark, and the Privy Council later approved the replacement of the feudal regime with universal voting rights and an elected 28-member assembly.[4]

Despite these reforms, disputes over the constitutional relationships between Sark, Brecqhou and the Crown continued through legal challenges and political conflict involving the Barclays, Sark authorities and the United Kingdom government.[1] The conflict has often reflected the tension between Brecqhou’s treatment as part of Sark’s feudal jurisdiction and its owners’ claims to a distinct political and legal status within the Bailiwick.[2][5]

==Fort Brecqhou==
Fort Brecqhou is a granite-clad, castle-like private residence designed by the British architect John Quinlan Terry for Sir David and Sir Frederick Barclay and built between 1994 and 1996.[1] The DiCamillo Companion describes it as “the largest house built in Britain in the last 200 years”, while *The Critic* similarly called it the largest house built in Britain for at least two centuries.[1][2] The building is laid out on a quadrangular plan in a castellated Gothic style, with circular turrets at each corner.[1] Its architectural details include large twisted chimneys in a Tudor-derived form associated with the reign of Henry VIII.[1]  

The project, including the house, gardens and outbuildings, was estimated to have cost about £90 million.[1] Contemporary commentary has emphasised the building’s fortress-like character, noting that it was constructed from two types of Spanish granite and positioned on a rocky crown of the island.[2] A history of the island by Peter J. Rivett describes the later Barclay ownership period as culminating in the construction of the “magnificent castle” known as Fort Brecqhou.[3] The house is recorded as a fully extant country house and remains in private family ownership rather than being a listed historic building.[1]
